name: Two Stacks (amortized O(1))
summary: Push onto an inbox stack. When you need the front, tip the whole inbox into an outbox — that single reversal turns LIFO into FIFO, and every element is moved at most once.
visualizationIntent: init: show two empty stacks side by side, the operation tape above and an empty results rail below. check: highlight which branch the current operation takes. update: a push drops a value onto the IN stack; a transfer lifts values one at a time from IN and drops them onto OUT in reverse order; a pop or peek reads the top of OUT. move: advance the cursor to the next operation. return: show the full list of operation results.

lineExplanations.3: The inbox stack — everything pushed lands here first.
lineExplanations.4: The outbox stack — the front of the queue lives on TOP of this one.
lineExplanations.5: Collects one result per operation, mirroring what LeetCode prints.
lineExplanations.6: Which operation in the sequence we're replaying.
lineExplanations.7: The name of the operation currently being executed.
lineExplanations.8: Counts how many elements have ever been transferred between the stacks.
lineExplanations.9: Replay every operation in the sequence, one at a time.
lineExplanations.12: Drop the value on top of the inbox — pushes never touch the outbox.
lineExplanations.13: push returns nothing, so record None to keep results aligned with operations.
lineExplanations.14: pop and peek both need the queue's FRONT element, which lives on the outbox.
lineExplanations.15: Only refill the outbox when it is completely empty — refilling early would scramble the order.
lineExplanations.16: Tip the inbox over: move elements one at a time while any remain.
lineExplanations.17: THE KEY MOVE: popping from the inbox and pushing to the outbox reverses the order, so the OLDEST element ends up on top.
lineExplanations.18: Count this transfer — each element is moved at most once, which is why the cost averages out to O(1).
lineExplanations.20: Remove and return the top of the outbox — that is the front of the queue.
lineExplanations.22: Read the top of the outbox WITHOUT removing it.
lineExplanations.24: The queue is empty only when BOTH stacks are empty.
lineExplanations.25: Move on to the next operation.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: is the class wrapper LeetCode expects.
syntaxExplanations.2: def processOperations(self, operations, values): replays a whole call sequence; operations holds the method names, values the argument for each.
syntaxExplanations.3: [] creates an empty list used as a stack — append pushes, pop() pops the top.
syntaxExplanations.4: A second empty list, used as the outbox stack.
syntaxExplanations.5: results collects the return value of every operation.
syntaxExplanations.6: op_index = 0 starts at the first operation.
syntaxExplanations.7: current_op = "" declares the variable before the loop uses it.
syntaxExplanations.8: moved = 0 counts transfers between the two stacks.
syntaxExplanations.9: while op_index < len(operations): replays each call in order.
syntaxExplanations.10: operations[op_index] reads the name of the current call.
syntaxExplanations.12: append(values[op_index]) pushes this call's argument onto the inbox.
syntaxExplanations.13: None is Python's null — push has no return value.
syntaxExplanations.14: 'or' is True when either side is True; both operations need the outbox loaded.
syntaxExplanations.15: len(out_stack) == 0 tests whether the outbox is empty.
syntaxExplanations.16: while len(in_stack) > 0: keeps moving until the inbox is drained.
syntaxExplanations.17: in_stack.pop() takes the TOP of the inbox; append puts it on the outbox.
syntaxExplanations.18: moved = moved + 1 counts one element transferred.
syntaxExplanations.19: Another string comparison, this time for pop.
syntaxExplanations.20: out_stack.pop() removes and returns the outbox's top element.
syntaxExplanations.21: Another string comparison, this time for peek.
syntaxExplanations.22: out_stack[len(out_stack) - 1] reads the last element without removing it.
syntaxExplanations.23: Another string comparison, this time for empty.
syntaxExplanations.24: 'and' is True only when both sides are True — both stacks must be empty.
syntaxExplanations.25: op_index = op_index + 1 advances to the next operation.
syntaxExplanations.26: return results hands back every operation's result.
